coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of carbon quantum dot-carbon nanotube composites on waste eggshell-derived catalysts for enhanced adsorption of methylene blue
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiang
Wang, Hong
Tang, Xiaodong
Ba, Zihan
Zhao, Xiaoqiang
Wang, Yi
Deng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Pristine CNTs always suffer from a slow adsorption rate or low adsorption capacity. Development of CNT-based adsorbents is highly desirable to improve their adsorption performance. In this study, novel carbon quantum dot-carbon nanotube composite (CQD-CNT) adsorbents and defective CNTs were synthesized for the first time by one-step chemical vapor deposition using catalysts derived from waste eggshells. The coverage of CQDs on CNTs endowed CQD-CNT with a larger surface area (324.4 m3/g), more abundant pore structures and surface functional groups compared to defective CNTs. The adsorption of methylene blue (MB) on CQD-CNT was systematically studied, and CQD-CNT demonstrated good adsorption capacity and extremely fast adsorption rate for MB at a wide pH range (2–12) and different temperatures (293–313 K). A high removal efficiency of 99.5% was achieved in 5 min, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 299.4 mg/g, which was 1.3 and 4.6 times higher than that of defective CNTs and pristine CNTs, respectively. The adsorption results were closely fitted by Langmuir isotherm model and pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT involved multiple adsorption mechanisms, including electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking interaction and pore filling. In addition, CQD-CNT showed good reusability and stability, retaining a removal efficiency of 95.1% after 5 cycles. Finally, high decolorization rates (98.6–100%) were obtained using CQD-CNT in the treatment of simulated textile wastewater. These results demonstrate that CQD-CNT benefitted from one-step green synthesis and the effective utilization of waste eggshells has potential applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic view of the synthesis of CNMs from waste eggshell-derived catalysts.
XRD profiles of as-synthesized CNMs and purified CNMs. CNMs (CNM-600 and CNM-700) were synthesized from the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst after catalyst reduction at 600 °C (a) and 700 °C (b), respectively.

(a, b) FESEM and TEM images of defective CNTs; (c, d) FESEM and TEM images of CQD-CNT; and (e, f) UV–vis absorption spectra of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. Insets are optical images of the supernatants used for UV–vis absorption.
(a, b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. (c) Raman spectra and (d) FTIR spectra of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT.

(a) Adsorption isotherms of MB on defective CNTs and CQD-CNT at different temperatures. (b) The effect of CQD-CNT dosage (250–1500 mg/L) on MB removal. pH = 6, T = 313 K, MB concentration = 100 mg/L, contact time = 5 min (c) The effect of initial pH on MB removal by CQD-CNT. T = 313 K, contact time = 5 min, adsorbent dose = 10 mg. The pH was adjusted by using HCl and NaOH. (d) The effect of contact time on the removal efficiency of MB by CQD-CNT at different initial concentrations of MB. pH = 6, T = 313 K, adsorbent dose = 10 mg.
(a) Pseudo-first-order kinetic model and (b) pseudo-second-order kinetic model of CQD-CNT for the adsorption of MB.

(a) The reusability of CQD-CNT for the adsorption of MB. pH = 6, T = 313 K, contact time = 5 min, Concentration of MB = 100 mg/L, adsorbent dose = 10 mg. (b) The stability of CQD-CNT analyzed by UV–vis absorption spectra of the filtrates after each cycle in the reusability experiments.

FTIR spectra of CQD-CNT before (a) and after (b) adsorption of MB (the first cycle) and pure MB (c). (d) The mechanism diagram of MB adsorption on CQD-CNT through electrostatic interaction (ⅰ), hydrogen bonding (ⅱ), π=π stacking interaction (ⅲ), and pore filling (ⅳ).
UV–vis absorption spectra of simulated textile wastewater A, B and C before (a) and after (b) CQD-CNT treatment.
Langmuir, Freundlich and D-R isotherm model data of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of carbon quantum dot-carbon nanotube composites on waste eggshell-derived catalysts for enhanced adsorption of methylene blue

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pristine CNTs always suffer from a slow adsorption rate or low adsorption capacity. Development of CNT-based adsorbents is highly desirable to improve their adsorption performance. In this study, novel carbon quantum dot-carbon nanotube composite (CQD-CNT) adsorbents and defective CNTs were synthesized for the first time by one-step chemical vapor deposition using catalysts derived from waste eggshells. The coverage of CQDs on CNTs endowed CQD-CNT with a larger surface area (324.4 m3/g), more abundant pore structures and surface functional groups compared to defective CNTs. The adsorption of methylene blue (MB) on CQD-CNT was systematically studied, and CQD-CNT demonstrated good adsorption capacity and extremely fast adsorption rate for MB at a wide pH range (2–12) and different temperatures (293–313 K). A high removal efficiency of 99.5% was achieved in 5 min, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 299.4 mg/g, which was 1.3 and 4.6 times higher than that of defective CNTs and pristine CNTs, respectively. The adsorption results were closely fitted by Langmuir isotherm model and pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT involved multiple adsorption mechanisms, including electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking interaction and pore filling. In addition, CQD-CNT showed good reusability and stability, retaining a removal efficiency of 95.1% after 5 cycles. Finally, high decolorization rates (98.6–100%) were obtained using CQD-CNT in the treatment of simulated textile wastewater. These results demonstrate that CQD-CNT benefitted from one-step green synthesis and the effective utilization of waste eggshells has potential applications in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, dyes are widely used in textile, paper, plastic, cosmetics and other industries, which have produced a large amount of dye wastewater [1]. In recent reports, more than 100,000 types of dye wastewater have been discharged into the environment [2]. Organic dyes, e.g. methylene blue (MB) are toxic and harmful to human health. Therefore, it is necessary to treat the dye wastewater before it is discharged into the water bodies. Numerous technologies, including membrane filtration [3,4], photocatalytic degradation [5] and adsorption [6–9], have been developed for wastewater treatment. Among these technologies, adsorption is considered as a promising method due to the simple process, low cost and high efficiency [10]. A variety of materials have been used as adsorbents for dye removal, including activated carbon (AC), biochar (BC), graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), silica, clay, polymer, etc. [10–15].

As the typical one-dimensional carbon nanomaterials (CNMs), CNTs have well-defined cylindrical hollow structures, high aspect ratios, easily modified surfaces and good thermal stability, which exhibit great potentials as effective adsorbents in dye wastewater treatment. Nevertheless, pristine CNTs always form aggregates due to the strong van der Waals interactions, which lead to CNTs’ limited specific surface area and inaccessible pore structures for adsorbate molecules [16]. In addition, pristine CNTs lack functional groups. All these factors result in CNTs’ slow adsorption rate or low adsorption capacity (< 100 mg/g). In order to improve the adsorption performance, various methods have been used to modify CNTs, which include oxidation of CNTs by strong acids (such as nitric acid and/or sulfuric acid) [17,18], KOH activation [19] or the formation of CNT-based adsorbents with other materials such as BC [20], metal-organic framework [21] and polyaniline [22]. These modification methods provided more adsorption sites on the external walls of CNTs, resulting in an improved adsorption performance, but the modification of CNTs always involves the severe chemical treatments by strong acids or alkalies, which would destroy the CNT structure. Furthermore, the extensive use of strong acid and alkaline is not environmentally friendly. In addition, the preparation of CNT-based adsorbents always includes cumbersome steps, such as CNT synthesis, modification, and the subsequent composited with other materials [21,22], which would usually increase the preparation cost.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally small carbon nanoparticles with a size less than 10 nm, which have the advantages of nanoscale size and abundant functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) on their surfaces [23]. Previous studies have found that the formation of carbon quantum dot-carbon nanotube (CQD-CNT) composites by coating CQDs on the CNT surface can introduce abundant functional groups on CNTs, which enhanced CNTs’ dispersibility and prevented their aggregation [24]. We infer that the preparation of a novel CQD-CNT composite adsorbent may remarkably improve CNTs’ adsorption performance. However, similar to other CNT-based composites, the preparation of CQD-CNT composites in previous reports involved multiple steps [25]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a one-step synthesis method for CQD-CNT composites with enhanced adsorption performance.

Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is the common method used for CNT synthesis due to its advantages of relatively mild operation conditions, easy control and mass production [26]. Catalysts used in CNT synthesis are usually composed of metal components and supports. Fe, Co, and Ni are mostly preferred active components required for CNT synthesis due to their relatively high carbon solubility and carbon diffusion rate, and their binary mixtures often display a higher activity than individual elements [27–29]. Common catalyst supports include porous materials such as Al2O3, MgO, SiO2 and CaO etc. [30]. Among these supports, CaO can be easily removed by dilute acid treatment, which is beneficial to obtain high-purity CNT products. Moreover, CaO is cost-effective and sustainable as it can be obtained from calcium-based wastes such as eggshells. Eggshells are produced in large amounts by worldwide consumption of eggs [31–33], in which 26.3 wt% were directly discarded as solid wastes in the landfills [34]. Eggshells possess porous structures and contain more than 90 wt% CaCO3, which have been used to prepare low-cost CaO catalysts to synthesize nanoporous graphene [35]. We envision that the effective use of eggshells in the synthesis of CNT-based adsorbents would not only reduce the synthesis cost, but also provide an environmentally friendly method to synthesize novel adsorbents for wastewater treatment.

Herein, this study reported for the first time one-step synthesis of CNT-based adsorbents via the CVD method using Fe-Co/CaO catalyst. Surprisingly, defective CNTs and CQD-CNT composites were selectively synthesized at different catalyst reduction temperatures. Defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were then characterized by multiple techniques. The adsorption capacity of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT was evaluated by using MB as a model dye pollutant. The effects of adsorbent dose, pH value, contact time, adsorption temperature and MB concentration on the removal of MB by CQD-CNT were investigated. Various kinetic, isotherm and thermodynamic models were used to study the adsorption mechanism. In addition, the reusability and stability of CQD-CNT were analyzed. The adsorption mechanism was also proposed to illustrate the adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT. Finally, the simulated textile wastewater was used to evaluate the adsorption performance of CQD-CNT in a complex system.

Waste eggshells were collected from school canteens and washed several times with deionized water to remove the inner shell membrane. Cobalt(Ⅱ) acetylacetone (C10H14CoO4, 97%) was purchased from Aladdin, and iron(Ⅲ) acetylacetone (C15H21FeO6, 99%) was purchased from Energy Chemical. Anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH, ≥ 99.7%), MB (C16H18ClN3S, ≥ 98.5), NaOH (AR), HCl (AR) were from Chengdu CHBON, 0.1 mol/L NaOH and 0.1 mol/L HCl were used in this study. All reagents were used without further purification.

Eggshells were washed with deionized water, dried and calcined at 900 °C for 3 h in a muffled furnace similar to a previous report [36]. After calcination, the obtained CaO was ground into powder, and stored at room temperature in a glass vial sealed with the paraffin film. The Fe-Co/CaO catalyst with a Fe/Co molar ratio of 1 was prepared by incipient wetness impregnation method [37,38]. The total metal loading was 5 wt%. Cobalt(Ⅱ) acetylacetone and iron(Ⅲ) acetylacetone were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (both have a molar concentration of 0.01 mol/L) and then added to CaO powder. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30 min, and then stirred at 60 °C until the mixture became viscous. Afterwards, the mixture was dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the dried catalyst was ground and calcined at 450 °C for 3 h.

CQD-CNT was synthesized by atmospheric pressure CVD. First, the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst (150 mg) was loaded in a ceramic boat at the center of a tube furnace. The catalyst was then reduced under flowing H2 (1 bar, 30 sccm) from room temperature to a reduction temperature of 700 °C at 10 °C/min. When the temperature reached at 700 °C, the reactor was purged with Ar, while its temperature was further raised to 850 °C. Next, the ethanol vapor was then introduced into the reactor by flowing Ar (500 sccm) for 30 min. Afterwards, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature in Ar. The as-synthesized sample was collected and labeled as CNM-700. For comparison, defective CNTs (also labeled as CNM-600) were also prepared using a similar synthesis procedure, but the reduction temperature of Fe-Co/CaO catalyst was shifted to 600 °C. To obtain purified CNMs, the as-synthesized CNMs were immersed in dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) for 2 h to remove the catalyst. The synthesis scheme is given in Fig. 1.

The as-synthesized CNMs and purified CNMs were first characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). XRD was carried out on a diffractometer (Rigaku Smartlab, Japan) from 5° to 80° with the 2θ step size of 0.02° and the counting time of 12 s, using Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. The morphology of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT was observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, GeminiSEM 300, ZEISS, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were obtained by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI TECNAI G2F20, USA). The samples were dispersed in ethanol by bath sonication and then dropped onto an ultra-thin carbon film for TEM analysis. UV–vis absorption spectra of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were obtained using a Shimadzu 1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The samples were dispersed in deionized water by bath sonication and then the supernatant was extracted by high-speed centrifugation (13,500 r/min) for analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (WQF-520, Beijing Rayleigh) to detect the functional groups of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. The pre-dried samples were mixed with dry KBr powders and then compressed into self-supporting wafers. Spectra were acquired at a resolution of 4.0 cm−1 in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 and averaged over 32 scans at room temperature. Defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were also characterized by Raman spectroscopy (ID Raman micro IM-52) in the backscattering configuration using a 785 nm laser. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured using a pore size analyzer (Quantachrome, Quadrasorb SI) at 77 K. Before measurement, the sample was degassed at 250 °C for 6 h under high vacuum (< 0.01 mbar). The specific surface areas (SSA) of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were calculated by the Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (BET) method. The pore size and pore size distribution were calculated by the Density Functional Theory (DFT) method, using the desorption branch of the isotherms.

The adsorption performance of CQD-CNT and defective CNTs was studied in batches by fixing the volume of MB solution at 10 mL and the magnetic stirring at 150 rpm. The effects of different pH values (2–10), adsorbent dose (0.25–1.5 g/L), contact time (1–30 min), adsorption temperature (293 K, 303 K, 313 K) and MB concentration (50–600 mg/L) on the adsorption of MB were investigated. The standard curve was plotted by measuring the standard MB solution with a series of concentration gradients. After the adsorption experiment, the concentration of MB left in the supernatant solution was determined by a UV–vis spectroscopy (Shimadzu, UV-1800) at 664 nm. The equilibrium adsorption capacity Qe (mg/g) and MB removal efficiency were calculated using the following formula, respectively:

After the adsorption experiment, regeneration of the adsorbent was further carried out by the solvent desorption and heat treatment, respectively. For the solvent desorption, the adsorbent was washed using an acidic ethanol solvent with pH = 2, and then dried for 12 h. In the case of heat treatment, the adsorbent was thermally annealed in Ar at 300 °C for 6 h. Five cycles of adsorption-desorption processes were successively conducted to test the reusability of CQD-CNT, and the filtrate in each cycle was obtained by filtration using mixed cellulose ester membrane with 0.2 µm pore size and then measured by the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy to test the stability of CQD-CNT. The simulated textile wastewater experiment was carried out under the following conditions: room temperature, natural pH, 10 mg CQD-CNT, 10 mL simulated wastewater, contact time of 60 min.

The structures of as-synthesized CNMs and purified CNMs were first characterized by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, XRD profiles of CNMs synthesized from the Fe-Co/CaO catalyst after catalyst reduction at 600 °C and 700 °C are similar with a series of diffraction peaks at 32.2°, 37.4°, 53.9°, 64.2°, 67.4°, 79.7°, which can be ascribed to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), and (400) planes of CaO (PDF# 48-1467) derived from eggshells, and the small diffraction peak at 44.9° is attributed to the Fe-Co alloy in the catalyst [39]. In addition, there are diffraction peaks at 26.2° and 42.8°, which indicated the presence of CNTs in both samples [40]. In contrast, the XRD profiles of purified CNMs mainly exhibit the diffraction peaks from CNTs, and the diffraction peaks from CaO disappeared, which indicated that CaO was removed during the sample purification.

The morphology of purified CNMs was analyzed by FESEM and TEM. As shown in the FESEM images of the purified CNM-600 ( Figs. 3a and S1a), multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) are randomly oriented with a diameter range of 20–30 nm and a length up to micrometers. The TEM image in Fig. 3b demonstrates that CNTs contained a number of defects, as indicated by the black arrows. Few CaO particles were observed in the FESEM images, indicating that the dilute acid treatment almost removed the CaO support, which is consistent with XRD results. Similar with purified CNM-600, the FESEM images in Figs. 3c and S1b show that purified CNM-700 consisted of random CNTs with a diameter of 20–40 nm, and CaO particles were not observed; however, it can be clearly observed that carbon nanoparticles are densely packed on the external walls of CNTs, and the size of individual carbon nanoparticles is less than 6 nm. Furthermore, the HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 3d shows that the size of carbon nanoparticles is around 3 nm, which is the characteristic size of CQDs. It can be also observed in Fig. 3d that CQDs are well dispersed and some of CQDs are attached on the CNTs surface (as indicated by the open circles). It should be noted that the sample was dispersed by sonication for TEM analysis, which may make some CQDs detached from the CNT surface. To further confirm the TEM results, purified CNMs were directly dispersed in deionized water by sonication and the black dispersions were then centrifuged to obtain the supernatant for UV–vis analysis. As shown in Fig. 3e, a colorless supernatant was collected after centrifugation of CNM-600 dispersion and no obvious absorption bands were observed in its UV–vis absorption spectrum. In contrast, a black supernatant was obtained from CNM-700 after the same procedures, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 3f demonstrates strong absorption peaks at 220 and 260 nm, which can be assigned to π–π* transition of CC [41], confirming the presence of CQDs in the sample. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that when the catalyst was reduced at 600 °C, the synthesized CNM was defective CNTs, whereas the catalyst after reduction at 700 °C produced CQD-CNT.

N2 physisorption experiments were further performed to characterize the pore structure of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. As shown in the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms ( Fig. 4a and b), both defective CNTs and CQD-CNT demonstrate combined isotherms of types I and IVa [42], suggesting the existence of micropores and mesopores in defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. The nearly vertical increase at the low relative pressure region also indicates that micropores are present in the CQD-CNT [43]. Pore size distribution shown in Fig. 4a demonstrates that defective CNTs mainly have mesopores at around 4 nm, whereas the pores of CQD-CNT have a narrow distribution of micropores at around 1.5 nm and a broad distribution of mesopores at 3–15 nm (Fig. 4b). The calculated BET surface areas of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT are 227 and 324.4 m2/g, respectively. N2 physisorption results indicated that the coverage of CQD on CNTs led to an increase of surface areas and the appearance of more pore structures in the CQD-CNT.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to characterize the structural changes of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. As shown in Fig. 4c, the spectrum shows D band and G band at 1340 and 1580 cm−1, respectively, which are characteristic Raman peaks of carbon nanostructures. The D band is attributed to the defects and disorders in carbonaceous solid, and G band is ascribed to the structurally ordered graphite crystallites. The degree of graphitization and the number of defects can be evaluated by the intensity ratio of G band to D band (IG/ID) [44]. The IG/ID value of defective CNTs is 0.52, suggesting that CNTs have a low degree of graphitization and a large number of defects [45], which is consistent with the TEM results. In contrast, the IG/ID value of CQD-CNT is 0.47, which is lower than defective CNTs, indicating that CQD-CNT has more defects than defective CNTs. This may be attributed to the presence of CQDs with numerous surface defects [46]. More defects usually provide more adsorption sites, and it is expected that CQD-CNT has a good adsorption capacity for MB.

The surface functional groups of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were studied by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4d, defective CNTs have a wide band around 3400 cm−1, which is attributed to the tensile vibration of OH [47]. The other bands around 2927, 1630, 1560, 1049 and 670 cm−1 belong to the deformation vibration of CH3, the stretching vibration of CC, CO, CO [19,48], and the bending vibration of CH [49], respectively. In contrast, CQD-CNT has more prominent bands at 3400 and 1630 cm−1. These surface functional groups would make the composites possess a certain degree of hydrophilicity and act as anchor sites for MB molecules.

Adsorption isotherms provide information on the capacity of the adsorbent as well as the interactions between adsorbate and adsorbent. In this study, the adsorption isotherms of MB on defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were carried out at 293, 303, and 313 K. As shown in Fig. 5a, adsorption capacities of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT both increased with the increase of the MB concentration; but the adsorption capacities of MB on CQD-CNT were much higher than those of defective CNTs. It was also observed that adsorption capacities of defective CNTs and CQD-CNT increased with the increase of temperature, which indicated that the adsorption of MB on defective CNTs and CQD-CNT were both endothermic.

The adsorption isotherm data were fitted by three adsorption isotherms models, namely Langmuir, Freundlich, and D-R isotherm model, as described in the Supplementary materials (S1). The fitting curves and relevant isotherm parameters of Langmuir, Freundlich, and D-R isotherm models are presented in Fig. S2a–f and Table 1. Evidently, Langmuir isotherm model has a higher correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.999) than Freundlich and D-R isotherm models, indicating that Langmuir isotherm model could best describe the adsorption of MB molecules on defective CNTs and CQD-CNT. Therefore, MB adsorption behaviors on defective CNTs and CQD-CNT both belong to the monolayer coverage on the adsorbent surface. Furthermore, the dimensionless constant separation factor (RL) is less than 1 under all experimental conditions in this study, which indicates that defective CNTs and CQD-CNT are both favorable for MB adsorption. The Qmax obtained by the Langmuir isotherm model are 227.3 and 298.5 mg/g for defective CNTs and CQD-CNT, respectively, which are very close to their experimental values of 228.2 and 299.4 mg/g. As CQD-CNT demonstrated an overall higher adsorption capacity than defective CNTs, the adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT was further investigated in detail.

The adsorbent dose is considered to be an important parameter for MB removal. The correlation between the adsorbent dose, adsorption capacity Qe and the removal efficiency is shown in Fig. 5b. As the adsorbent dose increased from 250 to 1000 mg/L, the removal efficiency increased from 53.5% to 98.4%, which was due to the increase of available adsorption sites [50]. However, Qe decreased from 214 to 98.4 mg/g with the increase of the adsorbent dose, which may be attributed to the relative decrease in the number of MB molecules on per unit of adsorbent. In addition, the increase in the adsorbent dose may also result in the aggregation of adsorbents, which caused some of the adsorption sites to overlap [51], reducing the adsorption capacity. Considering that the adsorbent dose of 1000 mg/L had a relatively high removal efficiency and adsorption capacity, the adsorbent dose of 1000 mg/L was used in the subsequent studies.

The effect of pH on the adsorption process was further investigated, and the result is shown in Fig. 5c. In general, CQD-CNT exhibited good removal efficiency in a wide pH range of 2–12, showing its potential to be used in various environments. Specifically, at a low pH value of 2, the removal efficiencies of CQD-CNT for the initial MB concentration of 50, 75, and 100 mg/L were 84.0%, 96.8%, and 99.1%, respectively, which increased with increasing pH and reached 100% in alkaline conditions (pH ≥ 8). This can be explained by static electricity [52]. In an alkaline environment, the strong electrostatic adsorption force exists between the negatively charged adsorbent and the cationic MB molecules. In contrast, in an acidic environment (pH < 6), H+ ions would compete with the MB molecules for the limited adsorption sites on the adsorbent, resulting in slightly lower removal efficiency. Considering the ease of operation, pH = 6 was selected in the adsorption experiments.

A rapid uptake of adsorbates and quick establishment of equilibrium imply the efficiency of an adsorbent for its use in wastewater treatment, and the contact time required to reach the equilibrium may be affected by the initial dye concentration. Therefore, the effect of contact time on the MB removal efficiency was investigated at three initial MB concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5d, during the initial adsorption stage, the removal efficiency of MB increased drastically within the contact time of 5 min. Meanwhile, the removal efficiency was also affected by the initial MB concentration. The removal efficiency decreased with the increase in the initial concentration of MB, which is attributed to more adsorption sites at low concentrations. As the contact time further increased, the removal efficiency increased slowly until a state of equilibrium reached, and the initial MB concentration had insignificant effects on the removal efficiency. This is a typical phenomenon of the adsorption process, which can be attributed to the decrease of adsorption sites on the adsorbent with the extension of contact time [50,53]. Specifically, the removal efficiencies within 5 min were found to be 99.5%, 98.6% and 98.4% at the initial MB concentrations of 50, 75 and 100 mg/L, respectively. And then the removal efficiencies increased very slowly with the contact time, achieving 100% removal efficiency at 10, 25 and 30 min, respectively. Thus, CQD-CNT showed extremely fast adsorption rates and high removal efficiencies for all the concentrations.

In order to evaluate the kinetic mechanism of the adsorption process, the experimental data were fitted by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, as described in the Supplementary materials (S2). The fitting curves and relevant kinetic parameters of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order are presented in Fig. 6 and Table S1. It can be seen that the experimental adsorption capacity (Qe,exp) is closer to the calculated adsorption capacity (Qe,1) obtained by the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, and the pseudo-first-order kinetics has a higher correlation coefficient (R2), which indicates that the pseudo-first-order kinetic model better describes the adsorption process.

In order to evaluate the influence of temperature on the adsorption process, the free energy change (ΔG0), enthalpy change (ΔH0) and entropy change (ΔS0) were calculated using the equations presented in Supplementary materials (S3). As listed in Table S2, the ΔG value is negative at all tested temperatures, indicating that the adsorption is spontaneous. And the value of ΔG decreases from −26.093 to −30.097 kJ/mol with the increase of temperature, which indicates that the adsorption process of MB on CNTs becomes more favorable at higher temperatures [54]. The values of ΔH0 and ΔS0 determined from the slope and intercept of the equation (ln(KL) = ΔS0/R − ΔH0/RT) are 32.58 kJ/mol and 201.16 J/(mol K)−1, respectively. A positive ΔH0 value indicates that the adsorption process was endothermic, which is consistent with the results obtained by the isothermal model, and its value less than 40 kJ/mol suggests that this was a physical adsorption process [55]. Furthermore, the positive value of ΔS0 indicates that CQD-CNT has a good affinity for MB, which facilitates the removal of MB.

The adsorption performance of CQD-CNT was compared with that of other CNT-based MB adsorbents reported in the literatures [56–64]. As presented in Table 2, CQD-CNT in this study showed a higher adsorption capacity than most of the other CNT-based adsorbents. Specially, the maximum adsorption capacity of CQD-CNT was 1.3 and 4.6 times higher than that of defective CNTs in this study and pristine CNTs reported in the literature [56], respectively. And it is also clear that the coating of CQD on CNTs remarkably increased the surface area of CNTs. Moreover, the equilibrium time is the shortest among those adsorbents, demonstrating the high efficiency of CQD-CNT for the MB adsorption in aqueous solutions. In addition, most CNT-based adsorbents were prepared by multiple steps, including the functionalization of CNTs by strong acid and alkalies and the subsequent combination of CNTs with other materials, which were based on CNT synthesis. In contrast, CQD-CNT was directly synthesized by a one-step method, avoiding the cumbersome steps and the extensive use of strong acid and alkalies, while also benefitting from improved sustainability associated with waste utilization.

Reusability and stability are crucial for practical applications of adsorbents. In this study, solvent desorption and thermal annealing were conducted to regenerate the adsorbent, respectively. As the phenomenon of desorption hysteresis was observed when the acidic ethanol solvent was used as eluent in the desorption experiments, the MB-adsorbed CQD-CNT was then regenerated by thermal annealing in Ar, and the reusability of CQD-CNT was further investigated through five cycles. As shown in Fig. 7a, the CQD-CNT retained a removal efficiency of 95% after five cycles, indicating that CQD-CNT has an excellent reusability for MB removal.

The good reusability of CQD-CNT may be attributed to its stability. In order to investigate the stability of CQD-CNT during the adsorption of MB, we conducted the UV–vis absorption analysis of the filtrates after each cycle in the reusability experiments. As shown in Fig. 7b, only the absorption peaks of MB appeared at 205, 244, 291, 610, and 662 nm, and no absorption peaks of CQDs were observed in each cycle, which confirmed the good stability of CQD-CNT during the adsorption of MB. It should be noted that the adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT was carried out at an agitation rate of 150 rpm, which is difficult to peel CQDs off from CNTs.

The adsorption mechanism was further proposed based on the above analysis and the FTIR spectra of CQD-CNT before and after MB adsorption, and the schematic diagram of adsorption mechanism is shown in Fig. 8, which involves the following aspects: (ⅰ) Electrostatic interaction. MB is a type of cationic dye which can be adsorbed easily by electrostatic forces on negatively charged surfaces. We have observed that the adsorption capacity of MB on CQD-CNT increases with the increase of pH, which is attributed to the increase of negative charges on the surface of CQD-CNT, indicating that electrostatic interaction is one of the adsorption mechanisms. We also compared the FTIR spectra of CQD-CNT and CQD-CNT-MB (after MB adsorption), which demonstrate the broadening of the band at 1049 cm−1 (CO) after MB adsorption, confirming the electrostatic attraction between CQD-CNT and MB [65]; (ⅱ) Hydrogen bonding. According to the FTIR spectra of CQD-CNT and CQD-CNT-MB, a shift is observed from 3444 to 3432 cm−1 for OH, which indicates that hydrogen bonding interaction may play a role in the MB adsorption onto CQD-CNT, as CQD-CNT contain oxygen-containing functional groups such as OH, and the H atom of OH can form hydrogen bonding with the S or N atoms of the CS and CN groups in MB molecules [19]; (ⅲ) π=π stacking interaction. Since MB is an ideally planar molecule, the π=π stacking interaction between CQD-CNT and MB molecules would also cause MB to be adsorbed on the surface of CQD-CNT [66], which can be confirmed by the decrease of the peak intensity of CH for CQD-CNT-MB as shown in the FTIR spectrum. (ⅳ) Pore filling. Based on the N2 adsorption-desorption result, CQD-CNT has a broad distribution of pore structures compared to defective CNTs, which may be due to the aggregation of spherical CQDs. Previous studies have demonstrated that closed interstitial spaces were formed in spherical fullerene aggregates, and adsorbate molecules would be entrapped in those closed interstitial spaces, which resulted in desorption hysteresis, while cylindrical CNTs could not form such closed interstitial spaces and thus no desorption hysteresis was observed [67]. In our study, the desorption hysteresis occurred during the regeneration of CQD-CNT using acidic ethanol. Thus, we believe that interstitial pore structures were formed in spherical CQDs aggregates, and the deformation and rearrangement of aggregates may lead to the infiltration of MB molecules into these pores during the adsorption process, which also contributes to the adsorption of MB.

Overall, the uniqueness of CQD-CNT lies in that the coverage of CQDs on the CNT surface endows CQD-CNT with an increased surface area and pore structures, which facilitates more MB molecules to contact the adsorption site efficiently; meanwhile, the presence of CQDs provides more oxygen-containing functional groups and π-π stacking interaction, thus leading to enhanced adsorption capacity and extremely fast adsorption rate for the adsorption of MB.

Textile wastewater usually contains a variety of dyes mixed with some inorganic salts. To investigate the application potential of CQD-CNT in the treatment of textile wastewater, we further configured simulated textile wastewater with different dye concentrations of 50, 100 and 200 mg/L labeled as A, B and C, respectively, and the detailed formulations of the simulated textile wastewater were shown in Table S3. The simulated textile wastewater was then treated by CQD-CNT, and the solutions before and after adsorption were analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 9a, strong absorption peaks of dyes in the simulated textile wastewater were observed in the UV–vis spectra, and the absorbance of those peaks remarkably decreased after adsorption by CQD-CNT (Fig. 9b). The decolorization rate was estimated by the integral method, which was listed in Table S3. It was observed that CQD-CNT demonstrated high decolorization rates of 100%, 98.65% and 99.88% for the simulated textile wastewater A, B and C, respectively. Therefore, CQD-CNT showed good adsorption performance in the treatment of complex textile wastewater. Although it was conducted in small-scale laboratory system, an upgrade of the experiment may enable this low-cost and environmentally friendly CQD-CNT to be used in actual situations. Meanwhile, the effective use of eggshells also provided a possible method for solid waste utilization.

Fe-Co/CaO catalysts were prepared by using waste eggshells as a catalyst support. Two types of CNMs, including defective CNTs and CQD-CNT, were selectively synthesized on Fe-Co/CaO catalysts via the CVD method by adjusting the catalyst reduction temperatures. Compared with defective CNTs, CQD-CNT has larger surface areas and more abundant pore structures and functional groups, resulting in higher MB adsorption capacity of 299.4 mg/g. Batch adsorption experiments revealed that adsorption on CQD-CNT was faster than that on other CNT-based adsorbent. Langmuir adsorption isotherm model and pseudo-first-order kinetics model effectively described the adsorption of MB on CQD-CNT. Thermodynamics analysis indicated that the adsorption process was spontaneous and endothermic. Furthermore, CQD-CNT demonstrated good reusability and stability. The adsorption mechanism was discussed, which involved multiple mechanisms including electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding, π=π stacking interaction and pore filling. In addition, CQD-CNT exhibited excellent decolorization rates of up to 100% in the simulated textile wastewater experiments, indicating its potential application in wastewater treatment. Compared to other CNT-based adsorbents reported in literatures, CQD-CNT prepared in this study has many advantages, such as one-step synthesis, environmentally friendly, high surface areas, good dye adsorption capacity and extremely fast adsorption rate. For future work, CQD-CNT can be further combined with other materials to form robust and multifunctional composites for broadening their applications in industry.

Qiang Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hong Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaodong Tang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Zihan Ba: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiaoqiang Zhao: Investigation. Yi Wang: Validation. Hong Deng: Funding acquisition, Project administration.
